title_en: Al-Bukhari and Al-Adab al-Mufrad: Biography and Background
title_ar: الإمام البخاري وكتاب الأدب المفرد: السيرة والخلفية
content_en:
Muhammad ibn Ismail al-Bukhari (810–870 CE / 194–256 AH) stands as the greatest hadith scholar in Islamic history, universally recognized for his monumental Sahih al-Bukhari. Yet alongside this masterwork, he compiled Al-Adab al-Mufrad — a separate collection devoted entirely to Islamic manners and social conduct — which reveals a different dimension of his scholarly vision.

Al-Bukhari was born in Bukhara, in present-day Uzbekistan, to a family with deep roots in Islamic scholarship. His father Ismail was himself a student of Imam Malik and died while al-Bukhari was still an infant. From childhood al-Bukhari displayed an extraordinary memory, reportedly memorizing over 70,000 hadiths with their complete chains of transmission. He began his travels in search of hadith at age sixteen, journeying throughout Khurasan, Iraq, the Hijaz, Syria, and Egypt over a period of sixteen years.

Al-Adab al-Mufrad was compiled after the completion of al-Jami as-Sahih, representing al-Bukhari's focused attention on a domain he considered insufficiently addressed in general hadith collections: the daily conduct, social ethics, and interpersonal manners that constitute the practical expression of Islamic character. The title translates roughly as 'A Unique Collection on Manners' — the word mufrad (unique, standalone) indicating that this was a self-contained work on a specific theme.

The collection contains approximately 1,322 hadiths organized into 644 chapters. Unlike the Sahih, where al-Bukhari applied his famously stringent authentication criteria — accepting only hadiths with unbroken chains of reliably transmitters who met with each other — the Adab al-Mufrad applies somewhat less restrictive standards. Al-Bukhari included hadiths of varying grades because the subject matter, while important, involves behavioral guidance rather than matters of obligatory ritual where only the highest-grade evidence suffices.

Scholars have debated this methodological difference. Some argue that al-Bukhari was following the established scholarly convention of being more lenient with hadiths on virtuous deeds (fada'il al-a'mal) and ethical conduct than with hadiths on legal rulings (ahkam). Others note that al-Bukhari was demonstrating that ethical refinement rests on an extensive prophetic tradition even when individual transmissions may be weaker than the standards of the Sahih.

The work was transmitted through al-Bukhari's direct students and eventually became one of the most referenced Islamic texts on manners. Its influence extended across all four legal schools and shaped the ethical literature of subsequent centuries.
